Finally, combined with the development trend of space technology, the application prospect of GaN devices in super-large satellite constellation power network, deep space exploration power system and space solar power station is prospected. This paper aims to provide reference for the research and development and engineering application of GaN devices in the field of space power supply, and promote the development of space power supply system in the direction of high efficiency, miniaturization and long life.

title: Upgrading LSTM-Attention to a Transformer Decoder for Image Captioning with Object and Visual Features
authors: Nima Sharifinia, Hasti Marjaneh, Mohammad Javad Parseh, Pejman Rasti
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Accurately generating natural language descriptions of visual scenes remains a core challenge in vision–language understanding. Existing image captioning models often entangle improvements in architecture, feature extraction, and large-scale pretraining, making it difficult to isolate the contribution of each component. In this work, we present a controlled architectural study that replaces a recurrent LSTM-attention decoder with a Transformer-based decoder while preserving the same visual feature inputs. The proposed framework fuses global context features from Xception and object-level embeddings from YOLO, ordered by detection confidence to emphasize salient visual entities. This design enhances long-range dependency modeling and cross-modal attention without introducing additional semantic or relational supervision. Trained solely on the MS-COCO dataset, the model achieves substantial improvements over a strong baseline—raising CIDEr by more than 100%—and produces captions that are both syntactically fluent and semantically grounded. These findings provide a reproducible and efficient foundation for future transformer-based captioning systems.

title: Interpretable Stacking Ensemble for Photovoltaic Power Forecasting under Extreme Low Irradiance
authors: Hanyang Guo, Weiru Wang, Xiaojuan Han
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Precise forecasting of photovoltaic (PV) power under extreme low-irradiance weather is essential for grid stability yet remains a formidable challenge due to complex nonlinearities. This paper proposes an interpretable Stacking ensemble framework that synergistically integrates prediction and explanation. The model employs a two-stage architecture, where six heterogeneous base learners are strategically combined via a meta-learner trained with cross-validation, effectively enhancing robustness and accuracy under diverse extreme conditions. Beyond achieving superior performance (average R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>>0.95, RMSE reduction>9.5% versus the best standalone model), the framework incorporates SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) to quantitatively interpret predictions. SHAP analysis reveals scenario-specific driving features—such as diffuse irradiance dominance during heavy rainfall—and deciphers nonlinear feature-power interactions. The proposed approach not only delivers a highly accurate forecasting tool but also provides actionable physical insights, offering a reliable and transparent decision-support solution for PV integration under increasingly volatile climates.

title: When Systolic Arrays Meet AI Engines: Architectural Constraints on AMD Versal ACAP
authors: Jason Kimko, Jason Cong
publish: 2026 IEEE 34th Annual International Symposium on Field-Programmable Custom Computing Machines (FCCM)
abstract: The AMD Versal ACAP provides a powerful heterogeneous architecture for accelerating kernels like general matrix multiplication (GEMM). Because the AI Engine (AIE) array mimics the spatial regularity of a systolic array (SA), mapping SA dataflows onto the fabric is highly intuitive. However, maximizing performance requires overcoming specific coordination challenges between data movement and compute cores. In this work, we demonstrate that the gap between theoretical peak and realized performance is the result of a fundamental "Systolic Tax" imposed by the AIE architecture. We provide a systematic study of 2D systolic mapping to quantify the architectural cost incurred when traditional wavefront data forwarding consumes execution cycles otherwise available for processing. This engine-level overhead leads to a simulated 8.2% compute cycle loss. To address this constraint, we implement a multicast-based SA (MCSA) design that leverages hardware multicasting for efficient operand delivery. This design is supported by a Mixed-Integer Non-Linear Programming model used for architectural optimization. Our design achieves a programmable logic frequency that analytically saturates the logic-engine communication bandwidth. While MCSA delivers a 1.28× throughput improvement over prior state-of-the-art systolic mappings, performance is ultimately limited by synchronization overhead, establishing a ceiling for systolic designs on these platforms and revealing the lack of parallel AIE-initiated data movement as a critical architectural bottleneck.

title: A Study on the Impact of Extraction Electrode Axes on Implant Dose in Applied Materials E500 Medium Current Implanter
authors: Yik Han Kwee, King Suen Lee, Ching Chee Chu
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: This study describes how the extraction electrode axes can have an adverse impact on the implant dose in the Applied Materials (AMAT) E500 medium current implanter. The extraction electrode, through its beam steering capability, influences the ion beam trajectory, which in turn affects the ion beam’s parallelism — a critical factor for accurate dose calculations during implantation in this implanter. Experiments were conducted using B⁺ implants at 200 keV under conditions of proper and improper extraction electrode axes tuning, with beam parallelism quantified using delta deflector voltage (DDV). DDV was found to be significantly higher under the improper axes tuning condition which indicated a greater degree of non-parallelism. Despite meeting the original equipment manufacturer (OEM) specifications for beam parallelism at 100 kV, the wafers implanted under improper tuning with B⁺ implants at 200 keV exhibited up to 5% overdose, through sheet resistivity measurements and dynamic secondary ion mass spectrometry (DSIMS) analysis. These findings highlight the importance of monitoring extraction electrode axes positions and DDV to ensure implant accuracy and minimize dose deviations. The findings also suggest that the OEM specifications for beam parallelism may be insufficient for higher energy implants and additional beam parallelism verification at larger energy ranges should be performed to ensure accurate ion implantation in the AMAT E500 medium current implanter.

title: DiffRouter: A Differentiable Routing Framework for UltraScale FPGAs
authors: Xiaohan Gao, Zhili Xiong, Yusu Wang, David Z. Pan
publish: 2026 IEEE 34th Annual International Symposium on Field-Programmable Custom Computing Machines (FCCM)
abstract: Routing has become a major bottleneck in large-scale FPGA design flows, where traditional routers rely on sequential net-by-net processing and rip-up-reroute heuristics. This inherent sequentialism limits parallelism, leads to suboptimal utilization of routing resources, and significantly increases runtime on modern FPGAs. This paper introduces DiffRouter, the first differentiable, gradient-based FPGA routing framework with GPU acceleration. By lifting routing into a continuous optimization space, DiffRouter enables highly concurrent routing and improved global optimality before projecting solutions back onto the discrete fabric. The framework consists of three key stages: (1) Resource-pruned preprocessing, which extracts compact routing variables per net to reduce memory footprint and problem complexity; (2) Differentiable global routing via gradient descent, which formulates routing as a Lagrangian-relaxed optimization that minimizes wirelength while iteratively enforcing connectivity and congestion constraints as relaxed terms; and (3) Postprocessing, which maps the continuous wire distributions to a valid UltraScale routing solution and completes solutions by detailed routing. Experiments on the FPGA 2024 Routing Contest benchmarks show that DiffRouter outperforms state-of-the-art parallel routers, achieving over 5× speedup over RWRoute and 2× over Vivado, and running 18% faster than the state-of-the-art academic FPGA router Potter while delivering competitive critical path wirelength.

title: Bidding Strategies for Multi-Type Charging Operators in Peak-Shaving Markets
authors: Shiyu Wang, Qinyi Xue, Qingyu Wu, Linjun Shi, Yang Li
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Electric vehicles are a significant controllable load resource, offering potential for grid regulation. EV types exhibit both regularity and uncertainty, so this study proposes separate data structure models for public and private charging stations, which are then aggregated. During day-ahead bidding for charging operators in peak-shaving ancillary services across various station types, a two-tier bidding-allocation model is proposed. This allows operators to participate in electricity ancillary services markets while also building these markets through flexible resource dispatch. Real-world simulation results show that the two-tier bidding-allocation strategy lowers net operating costs for charging operators and improves power mobilisation at public transport charging stations.

title: A Transfer Learning Modeling Framework Fusing Domain Knowledge and Its Application in District Heating Network
authors: Liang Zhang, Hui Zhang, Bo Sun, Jing Chen, Xiangyu Zhou
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: District heating networks (DHNs) represent a critical infrastructure for decarbonizing the heating sector. However, as DHN systems grow in scale and complexity, the computational burden associated with numerical simulations increases significantly. To meet both user demand and energy efficiency objectives under emission reduction policies, efficient and accurate simulation modeling approaches are essential for the optimal operation and scheduling of DHNs. This paper proposes a transfer learning-based modeling framework integrated with domain knowledge to enable high-fidelity network modeling. First, the partial differential equation governing the dynamic temperature evolution of fluid in heating pipelines is incorporated into a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) as embedded domain knowledge. Subsequently, high-resolution data generated by Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulations are employed to construct a pre-trained model for an individual heating pipe. Finally, this pre-trained model is fine-tuned according to the specific initial and boundary conditions of various pipelines within the district heating network, enabling accurate knowledge transfer through integration with sparse measurement data. Experimental results show that the proposed framework agrees well with reference numerical solutions in the literature and exhibits superior accuracy compared to conventional neural network–based modeling approaches.

title: Research on Integrated AC/DC Shore Power Supply based on DC Bus
authors: Mengtao Zheng, Yifei Yang, Minjia Tan, Zhenfei Wu, Jianpeng Zhang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: To address the need for coexisting AC and DC power supply for various ships in port shore power, this paper proposes an AC-DC integrated shore power system based on a DC bus, ensuring stable DC bus voltage and high-quality AC voltage and current output. A dual closed-loop control strategy for voltage and current based on a synchronous rotating coordinate system has been designed to achieve stable voltage of the DC bus and high-quality voltage and current output on the AC side. Simulation verifies its steady-state and dynamic performance under load changes. Experiments on a low-voltage prototype test AC and DC outputs, showing good power quality and stability, meeting diverse ships' direct and AC shore power demands.

title: Correlation between particles and induced voids at the bonding interface
authors: Vasco Matias, Hans Billington, Soheila Bagherilimaei, Serena Iacovo, Soon Aik Chew
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: Contamination particles induce voids at the bonding interfaces in wafer-to-wafer (W2W) and die-to-wafer (D2W) hybrid bonding (HB). Such bonding voids can be detrimental to the electrical properties of the devices being fabricated.In this study we have deposited W-particles with e-beam assisted deposition on the surface of SiCN-coated wafers, proceeded to bonding the wafers with a blanket counterpart and inspected the resulting bonding voids with a Scanning Acoustic Microscope.We could obtain a correlation between the sizes of the deposited particles and the respective induced voids – an amplification factor of 50x. Such correlation allows determining the sensitivity of bonding voids to the dimensions of particles at the bonding interface.

title: A novel approach for controlling robotic eye movement using U-Net based model
authors: Reza Javanmard Alitappeh, Saeed Abbasi, Mohammad Reza Jafari, Mahyar Eslamian
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: This paper proposes a simplified vision-based approach to the control of robotic eye systems using deep learning. Instead of complex physical modeling and nonlinear control equations, desired eye movements are encoded as 2D images, and a U-Net encoder predicts corresponding motor control signals. We present a model that has a CNN encoder, multilayer perceptron, and smoothing module that generates six motor signals for a 6-DoF cable-driven robotic eye. Trained on 199 trajectories in simulation, a system based on the proposed approach achieved a validation loss of 0.0075 (MSE) and rendered smooth, biologically plausible movements at reduced computational cost. While there is still some residual noise, our results indicate that trajectory images can be an effective representation of motion dynamics and an efficient alternative to physics-based models. The work described here points out the potential of image-driven control to simplify robotic eye design and lays the foundation for future real-time implementations on physical hardware.

title: Research on Electric Field Distribution of Composite Insulators under Different Extreme Weather Conditions
authors: Shicong Sun, Xuchen Lu, Yi Lin, Guanhua Li, Quanying Liu, Jingwei Han, Zhenchi Zhang, Bingbing Yu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Extreme weather associated with the northeast cold vortex tends to alter the surface conditions of composite insulators, thereby distorting their electric field distribution and threatening the safe and stable operation of transmission lines. Taking 500 kV high-voltage composite insulators as the research object, this paper establishes multi-physics coupling simulation models under three typical extreme weather conditions using COMSOL Multiphysics. The influence laws of different weather conditions on the electric field intensity in various directions and the surface electric field distortion of insulators are systematically analyzed. Key regional electric field parameters are extracted through simulation to clarify the influence degree of different extreme weather events, and the correlation between electric field distortion characteristics and operation risks is analyzed. The results show that short-duration heavy precipitation and rainstorm with strong wind exert the most significant distortion effect on the electric field distribution of insulators. The uniformity of electric field distribution is relatively poor under hail weather, and the edges of insulator sheds are always the high-incidence areas of electric field concentration. The research results provide theoretical support and data reference for the insulation design, risk assessment of composite insulators and safety protection of transmission lines under extreme weather, and possess important engineering value for improving the adaptability of high-voltage transmission lines to extreme weather.

title: Intelligent Model for Identifying Defective Aircraft Parts
authors: Zohre Fasihfar, Hamidreza Rokhsati, Hossein Lotfi, Peyman Vafadoost Sabzevar
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: The aviation industry heavily relies on the safety and reliability of aircraft parts. Detecting and addressing defective parts promptly is crucial to ensure the overall safety of aircraft operations. In this paper, we propose an AI-enhanced Extreme Learning Machine (ELM) model for the identification of defective aircraft parts. Our objectives include developing a comprehensive dataset of aircraft parts with labeled defect samples, training the ELM model using this dataset, integrating real-time monitoring for proactive defect detection, and creating a user-friendly interface for aviation professionals to interact with the system. Our methodology involves collecting a diverse dataset of aircraft parts with annotated defect labels, preprocessing the data, training the ELM model, incorporating sensor data for real-time monitoring, and developing an intuitive interface for users to visualize and analyze identified defects. The model achieves an impressive accuracy rate of 94.27% in accurately detecting and classifying defects in aircraft components. The high accuracy achieved by our model demonstrates its effectiveness in identifying defective aircraft parts and contributes to enhanced aviation safety and maintenance efficiency. Ongoing evaluation and validation of the model's performance are essential to ensure its continued accuracy and reliability in real-world scenarios. The successful implementation of our AI-enhanced ELM model has the potential to revolutionize the identification of defective aircraft parts, leading to safer and more reliable aviation operations.

title: Convolutional Estimation of Reactive Betweenness and Dependency from DC Load Flow Data
authors: Mohammad Shahraeini
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: This paper introduces a lightweight U-Net that estimates the full reactive dependency matrix D<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">Q</inf> from the DC dependency matrix D<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">dc</inf> with R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> =0.985, RMSE = 0.0198, and MAE = 0.0097 on masked entries of the IEEE 14-bus system. Using a direct input skip-addition and standard MSE loss, the CNN accurately captures reactive flow mediation patterns from computationally inexpensive DC load flow inputs. Post-processing thresholding eliminates small negative artifacts, ensuring physical consistency. Results on 50 unseen scenarios confirm near-perfect reconstruction of D<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">Q</inf>, enabling fast reactive power flow analysis without full AC load flow. The approach offers a scalable, interpretable, and real-time AI-driven solution for modern power system analytics.

title: Cu Via Resistance Failure Induced by CMP in Advanced Interconnects
authors: Wong Kian Hui, Leong Voon Kiong, Tubati Sricharan, Peck Yan Zheng
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: Elevated electrical via resistance in copper (Cu) top vias is attributed to insufficient CU removal during Platen 2 (P2) Chemical Mechanical Planarization (CMP), which is responsible for soft Cu landing and stops at Ta barrier by endpoint detection. Failure analysis based on Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) identified barrier metal cracking, Cu voids, and Cu migration from top via (TV) into the top metal (TM). Simulations confirm that reduced polishing from P2 process results in residual Cu remaining and subsequently induced a longer higher frictional force during barrier polishing on Platen 3 (P3), causing microstructural damage at the Cu/barrier interface. These defects, exacerbated by subsequent high-temperature processes, compromise via integrity and further increase resistance. The study highlights the need for optimized CMP endpoint control and uniform Cu removal to ensure interconnect reliability in advanced semiconductor fabrication.

title: Compliant Magnetic Sensor Arrays Enable Real-Time Force Myogram Pattern Recognition for Dexterous Hand Control by Amputees
authors: Wen-Yu Cheng, Erik D. Engeberg
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: Control of powered prosthetic hands could benefit from improved techniques to infer the amputees’ desired grasp intentions. Force myography (FMG) has recently emerged as a potential alternative to electromyography, which is traditionally used in clinical practice. Thus, we introduce innovative compliant magnetic FMG sensor arrays for forearm muscle pattern recognition that enables ten subjects, including three upper limb amputees, to have real-time control of a dexterous artificial hand. Sensor arrays with 18 or 24 compliant magnetic FMG sensor modules are fabricated, demonstrating the customizability of our 3D scanning—3D printing process to create individualized wearable sensor arrays for varying limb differences. On average, all 10 subjects control 16.20 ± 3.68 classes with mean accuracy of 93.64% ± 2.90% in real-time control experiments. Additionally, principal component analysis (PCA) is used to both select the most impactful sensors and improve classification accuracy in subsequent offline analyses. Seven out of the ten subjects achieve their highest accuracy with a reduced number of compliant magnetic sensors, highlighting the value of the PCA-informed sensor selection approach. Because the proposed compliant magnetic sensor arrays are highly practical and have a signal to noise ratio (SNR) of 34.51 dB ± 1.49 dB, we are providing our open-source dataset of FMG signals from 3 amputees and 7 non-amputees as a resource for the research community. These novel compliant magnetic FMG sensor arrays have the potential to advance the state of the art for prosthetic hand control and could be used broadly in the fields of tactile sensing, haptics, medical robotics, and rehabilitation.

title: An RGB-Event Hierarchical Fusion Enhancement Network for Object Detection
authors: Kaiyi Wang, Xinyu Hou, Siying Liu, Chen Cheng, Lei Deng, Hao Guo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Object detection in frame-based vision systems often faces the problem of significant performance degradation under challenging conditions due to the inherent sensing limitations of conventional cameras. Event cameras, with their advantages of high temporal resolution and sparse data output, offer a promising solution for addressing the performance degradation problem. However, due to the asynchronous and sparse nature of event data, event camera–based detection methods generally exhibit lower accuracy than their image-based counterparts. Therefore, effectively fusing data from both event and frame-based cameras represents the main challenge. To this end, this study introduces a hierarchical fusion enhancement network (FENET) designed to achieve more robust object detection through complementary multimodal fusion. First, to address the problem of underutilization of event timescales in conventional dense representations, this study designs an innovative, learnable multi-timescale event preprocessing module. This module generates temporal aggregation frames (TAFs) by leveraging event data from a spectrum of intervals, ranging from standard RGB exposure times down to finer timescales. This approach ensures the comprehensive capture and clear delineation of the target's structure, thereby enhancing the integrity of the visual information. Furthermore, a two-branch adaptive enhanced cross-modal aggregation module (ECAM), which guides the adaptive fusion of features from both modalities by first self-enhancing the features of each, is employed. The main goal of the ECAM is to achieve efficient feature aggregation by combining global context with local spatial information, subsequently using the aggregated features to steer the adaptive fusion process. Finally, by recognizing the scarcity of large-scale, high-quality annotated datasets for event-based object detection, the DSEC-D dataset comprising 60 sequences, with a total of 70,739 images and over 250,000 labels, is manually constructed. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that the proposed FENET can successfully overcome the perceptual limitations of conventional cameras. Notably, the proposed FENET surpasses current RGB-event fusion schemes, achieving an 8.6% improvement in the mAP50 metric and a 3.7% improvement in the mAP50:95 metric over the FRN baseline. We have released the code and the dataset at https://github.com/wkydawang/FENET.

title: Message Passing Based Bayesian Detection for Distributed Dual-Channel Passive MIMO Radar
authors: Bin Li, Jun Li, Qinghua Guo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: This paper investigates Bayesian target detection for distributed dual-channel passive multi-input multi-output (MIMO) radar to systematically exploit prior statistical knowledge of unknown parameters. First, a closed-form Bayesian generalized likelihood ratio test (B-GLRT) detector is derived by marginalizing random channel coefficients while estimating the unknown transmitted signals via maximum likelihood estimation, yielding a computationally efficient detector. Second, to further incorporate the discrete constellation structure of the transmitted symbols, a message passing based maximum-a-posteriori (MP-MAP) ratio test detector is proposed. It is efficiently realized via an iterative MP algorithm to jointly infer the channel coefficients, transformed signals and symbols, without exhaustive searching over all possible symbol sequences. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed detectors consistently outperform existing adaptive detectors across a wide range of signal-to-noise ratios.

title: Transmission Cross Section of Electrically Large Dielectric Interface
authors: Chong Li, Tieying Feng, Yongjiu Zhao, Lei Xing, Qian Xu, Yi Huang
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: The transmission cross section (TCS) of an electrically large dielectric interface is evaluated. By computing the plane wave reflection coefficients from both sides of the aperture, the corresponding TCSs are obtained using numerical integral. It is interesting to find that the average TCS can be derived analytically, and the ratio of the average TCSs from two sides of the interface equals the ratio of the dielectric permittivities. Both integral representations and analytical expressions of the average TCS are derived, and full-wave simulations are conducted in dielectric-filled reverberation chambers (RCs) for validation.

title: Optical Simulation & Image Quality for Automotive Semantic Segmentation
authors: Daniel Jakab, Joel Herrera Vázquez, Mahendar Kumbham, Brian Deegan, Tim Brophy, Reenu Mohandas, Anthony Scanlan, Enda Ward, Fiachra Collins, Ciarán Eising
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Vehicular Technology
abstract: Cameras are often deployed in automated driving systems for multiple perception tasks, including object recognition. In previous work, a novel optical simulation framework, SOLAS, was proposed to simulate wide-angle and fisheye cameras on automotive data for 2D object detection. The main focus of this work is to assess semantic segmentation networks in relation to camera quality, mainly used for collision avoidance, lane marking detection, and enhanced road navigation. Characterising semantic segmentation performance across the full range of camera production tolerances is essential, given that image quality may vary across units. This study further develops the novel optical simulation framework, SOLAS, where a Pythonic ray-tracing library, KrakenOS, is adapted to simulate wide-angle surround-view optical systems across a range of optical defocus, directly in automotive scenes. Optical defocus is achieved through sensor displacement. In this paper, we propose extending SOLAS with ARDÁN, a pipeline for measuring image quality in automotive scenes. Furthermore, we demonstrate that our framework, utilising a diverse set of cameras and dense grids of Point Spread Functions (PSFs), is scalable in semantic segmentation, achieving consistent results across various datasets supported by statistical metrics. The primary metric used for performance evaluation is mean Intersection over Union (mIoU). Based on our results, the Segformer network with 24.73M parameters was the most robust across wide-angle automotive datasets, namely, nuScenes and Virtual KITTI 2.0. In nuScenes, image sharpness (cycles/pixel) dropped by 20.5% from a nominal focus, with a small Segformer performance drop from 55.21% mIoU by 0.37%. In Virtual KITTI 2.0, image sharpness dropped by 44.66% from a nominal focus, with a Segformer performance drop by 37.42%. However, for fisheye, the PIDNet model with 28.76M parameters was the most robust model across the defocus range. In Woodscape, image sharpness dropped by 53.45% from a nominal focus, with a PIDNet performance drop by 8.55%. In Parallel Domain Woodscape, image sharpness dropped by 34.46% from a nominal focus, with a PIDNet performance drop by 7.28%.

title: Manipulation of Deformable Objects with Soft Grippers Based on Adaptive Fuzzy Control and a Self-Supervised Feature Descriptor
authors: Yuexuan Xu, Ning Han, Jinrui Li, Tong Yang, Yun-Hui Liu, Ning Sun, David Navarro-Alarcon
publish: IEEE Transactions on Fuzzy Systems
abstract: Although visual servoing control is widely used in practical applications, its application to deformable object manipulation (DOM) remains underexplored. This is due to the significant diversity of material properties and geometric configurations, the high uncertainties of deformation characteristics, and the high-dimensional configuration spaces. To this end, a new data-driven visual servoing control method is proposed for three-dimensional DOM. Specifically, a self-supervised keypoint detection network is developed without manual annotation while preserving spatial geometric information, addressing limitations of traditional dimensionality reduction methods. The network employs weighted feature aggregation modules to effectively balance the fusion of diverse local information while preserving features essential for keypoint localization. Furthermore, a geodesic distance loss function ensures the semantic consistency of detected keypoints across deformation processes. Based on the detected keypoints, an adaptive fuzzy-based Jacobian estimator is developed to establish the online mapping relationship between keypoint velocities and robot joint velocities. To optimize transient performance, a prescribed performance control method is designed to ensure that keypoint errors converge within predefined spatial funnel functions. Additionally, a pneumatic soft gripper based on a rigid origami-inspired mechanism is designed to prevent damage to deformable objects and provide compliant interaction. Finally, detailed stability analysis and a series of experimental results are provided to verify the feasibility of the proposed method.

title:
High-Performance Mid-Infrared Photodetector Based on WSe
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    /WTe
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    /WS
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    P-Type-Semimetal-N-Type Heterojunction
authors: Preeti Yadav, Anuj K. Sharma, Vandana Devi Wangkheirakpam
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: A high- performance PD comprising p-type WSe<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>/T<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">d</sub>-WTe<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> semi-metal/n-type WS<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> (PSN) configuration is reported by sandwiching 2D semi-metal between two semiconductor layers. In this heterojunction configuration, the top and bottom semiconductor layers generate an internal built-in electric field oriented vertically within the T<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">d</sub>-WTe<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>, which serves as an absorption layer for low energy photons leading to reduced dark current and enhanced collection efficiency. Finite difference time domain (FDTD) is used for simulation of proposed PD design. Notably, the simulated device can respond to the wide spectrum of 8-12 μm with the optimal wavelength around 9.68763 μm. In particular, the device demonstrated remarkable photovoltaic performance, characterized by a maximum responsivity of 4.56 A/W, a detectivity of 2.108 ×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">12</sup> Jones, and a low dark current of∼10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−17</sup> A at 9.687 μm. Further, the transient analysis led to rise and fall times of 5.29 μs and 4.48 μs, respectively, indicating a fast-responding PD design. Furthermore, the 3dB bandwidth of the proposed PSN design is calculated to be 66.16 kHz. This work presents a major step towards Weyl semimetal-based PDs on the silicon platform for MIR applications.

title: DeGMix: Efficient Multi-Task Dense Prediction with Deformable and Gating Mixer
authors: Yangyang Xu, Yibo Yang, Bernard Ghanem, Lefei Zhang, Bo Du, Jun Zhu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Convolution neural networks (CNNs) and Transformers offer complementary strengths for multi-task dense prediction: CNNs are effective at modeling local spatial patterns, while Transformers capture long-range dependencies. However, existing methods often rely primarily on one paradigm or do not fully exploit their complementarity for task-aware representation learning. In this work, we propose DeGMix (Deformable and Gating Mixer), a unified encoder–decoder framework for multi-task dense prediction that combines deformable convolution and attention-based task interaction. DeGMix consists of a deformable mixer encoder for task-aware spatial feature extraction and a task-aware gating Transformer decoder for cross-task interaction and task-specific feature selection. In this way, the proposed framework jointly models local deformable cues, global dependencies, and task-aware feature adaptation within a single architecture. Extensive experiments on three benchmark datasets, including NYUD-v2, PASCAL-Context, and Cityscapes, show that DeGMix consistently outperforms competitive CNN-based and Transformer-based multi-task methods across multiple dense prediction tasks. For example, with a Swin-L backbone, DeGMix achieves 57.55 mIoU on semantic segmentation on NYUD-v2, surpassing the previous best result by +5.2 mIoU. In parameterefficient fine-tuning on PASCAL-Context, DeGMix obtains a +1.96 Δm gain with 2.2M trainable parameters, approaching MTL-full fine-tuning (+2.23 Δm) while updating 13.7× fewer parameters. Our code and models are publicly available at https://github.com/yangyangxu0/DeGMix.

title: Thermo-Mechanical Frequency Stability and Anisotropic Modeling of Carbon Fiber-Reinforced PETG Accelerometers for Additive Manufacturing
authors: Xuan Luc Le, ThuHang Bui, Tuan Vu Quoc
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: This research presents a rigorous investigation into the structural optimisation and material-driven performance enhancement of 3D-printed inertial transducers, specifically focusing on the transition from monolithic thermoplastics to carbon fibre-reinforced composites (PETG-CF). Conventional Polyethylene Terephthalate Glycol (PETG) has been demonstrated functional viability under ambient conditions; however, its susceptibility to temperature-induced mechanical fluctuations and viscoelastic degradation remains a barrier to precision applications. The present study employs integrated modal, harmonic, and thermo-mechanical Finite Element Method (FEM) analysis to delineate the relationship between matrix reinforcement and operational stability across an extended thermal gradient from -40 °C to 100 °C. The analytical model yielded resonant frequencies at ambient temperature of 62.38 Hz for PETG and 102.62 Hz for PETG-CF, demonstrating a high correlation with FEM simulation results of 62.94 Hz and 103.71 Hz, respectively. The simulation results indicate that standard PETG-based transducers exhibit high thermal sensitivity, with the fundamental natural frequency decreasing from 67.1 Hz at −40 °C to 47.0 Hz at 75 °C, representing a linear frequency drift of approximately 0.32 Hz/°C. Conversely, the incorporation of carbon reinforcement (PETG-CF) significantly enhances mechanical resonance performance, achieving a fundamental natural frequency of 103.71 Hz at 25 °C, corresponding to a 65% increase over the baseline (62.945 Hz). The PETG-CF variant demonstrates superior thermal robustness, evidenced by a reduced average frequency shift of approximately 0.26 Hz/°C. The experimental validation of these findings was conducted through the utilisation of free-decay vibration measurements, which substantiated the observations. The resonant frequencies observed were found to be approximately 71 Hz for PETG and 106 Hz for PETG-CF. Static calibration yielded a sensitivity of approximately 108 mV/g. The results obtained establish a practical operational temperature limit between -40 °C and 75 °C, thus providing a fundamental framework for developing high-performance, low-cost micro-sensors through advanced Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) techniques.

title: Physically-Based Polarization Reflection Separation for Laser Stripe Extraction on Highly Reflective Metal Surfaces_supp2-3699730.mp4
authors: Yiting Yu
publish: N/A
abstract: Polarization-based laser vision imaging offers high accuracy, non-contact sensing, and strong robustness to interference, making it promising for weld seam localization and intelligent welding. However, for planar fillet-weld workpieces with highly reflective metallic surfaces, reliable laser stripe segmentation and precise extraction of centerlines and feature points remain challenging. This is because inter-reflection interference and camera saturation severely degrade measurement accuracy. In this paper, we propose a novel physically-based polarization reflection separation method using polarization features and spatial structural information for robust laser stripe extraction. The physical principles and polarization properties of direct- and inter-reflections from metallic surfaces are investigated through theoretical modeling and simulation. By combining the Stokes parameter S2 with a saturation region segmentation criterion, the inter-reflection regions of the laser stripe are automatically determined. An adaptive mask dilation approach is then introduced to suppress saturation-induced interference without complex parameter tuning. Finally, the centerlines and feature points are accurately extracted using the gray-gravity method with least-squares fitting. Experiments demonstrate superior segmentation and extraction performance under strong reflective interference, achieving an average feature-point extraction error of 2.36 pixels, a repeatability error within ±1.16 pixels, and an average computational time of 0.2531 s. Moreover, comprehensive evaluations across varying groove angles, laser rotation angles, exposure times, surface conditions and dynamic robotic platforms further validate the robustness and effectiveness of the proposed method. This study highlights the potential of polarization imaging for addressing optical interference challenges in industrial welding scenarios, providing a solid foundation for intelligent and automated welding.

title:
Adaptive Cross Section Optimization Method for Oversized Circular TE
                    <sub>01</sub>
                    -Mode Waveguide Bends
authors: Zhipeng Tu, Xiaoyi Liao, Ran Zhang, Guo Liu, Wei Jiang, Jianxun Wang, Yong Luo, Zewei Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: This article proposes a synthesis method for highly efficient, broadband, and compact circular TE<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">01</sub>-mode waveguide bends based on adaptive optimization of the waveguide cross section. To enable convenient parametric adjustment of the waveguide cross section while meeting the structural continuity and smoothness requirements for practical high-power applications, a construction method for waveguide cross sections based on Gaussian radial basis function (GRBF) superposition is introduced. For the sake of achieving an optimized cross section for efficient TE<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">01</sub> mode transmission, an improved particle swarm optimization (PSO) algorithm incorporating shape-family clustering, cross-family weak competition, and geometry-weighted parameter scaling is proposed. Based on the proposed method, a Ka-band 90° high-power TE<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">01</sub> mode waveguide bend was designed, fabricated, and tested. Simulation results show that the bend achieves a transmission efficiency above 95% and a reflection coefficient below −35dB across the 18.5–40.3 GHz frequency range. Near-field measurements further confirm an output mode purity exceeding 98%. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach in realizing high-efficiency, high power-capacity, broadband, and compact circular TE<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">01</sub> mode bending, offering a new technical reference for the development of high-performance high-power microwave devices.

title: RIS-Assisted Atomic MIMO Receiver
authors: Qihao Peng, Jiuyu Liu, Qu Luo, Yi Ma, Pei Xiao, Maged Elkashlan, George K. Karagiannidis
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: In this paper, we propose a novel and low-complexity atomic multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) receiver architecture assisted by a reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS). By introducing RIS and utilizing pulse amplitude modulation (PAM), the phase of the transmitted signal is effectively aligned with that of the local oscillator (LO), mitigating phase ambiguity and reducing the complexity of the receiver. To tackle the resulting non-convex optimization problem, we reformulate it into a tractable form by minimizing the Frobenius norm of an equivalent matrix, which is solved using an Adam-based gradient descent algorithm. Building upon the optimized equivalent signals, we develop a low-complexity signal detection algorithm and evaluate its performance. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed RIS-assisted atomic MIMO receiver significantly enhances detection performance compared to the conventional atomic MIMO receivers. More importantly, these results underscore the proposed architecture's potential for scalable and high-performance atomic MIMO communication systems.

title: LegoBot: Modular, Compact, Device-Agnostic Robot for Endovascular Catheter and Guidewire Manipulation in Stroke Thrombectomy
authors: Weibo Gao, Nitin Srinivasan, Dongming Piao, Qianhe Fan, Miki Guo, Rui Li, Peter Patalano, Hao Su
publish: IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics
abstract: State-of-the-art endovascular robots are often limited by coupled translation–rotation actuation, narrow compatibility with off-the-shelf devices, and fixed architectures that are difficult to reconfigure for multidevice procedures. These limitations are especially restrictive in neurovascular intervention, where long working lengths, tortuous anatomy, and coordinated manipulation of multiple catheters and guidewires are frequently required. In this work, we present LegoBot, a modular, compact, device-agnostic robot for neurovascular catheter and guidewire manipulation. First, we design a decoupled two-degree-of-freedom (2-DOF) actuation mechanism together with an instrument-agnostic adjustable clamping mechanism, enabling independent or simultaneous instrument translation and rotation while accommodating off-the-shelf devices with diameters ranging from 0.33 to 3 mm. Second, we present the mechatronic design of a modular catheter robot with interchangeable adapters for different instruments, which support module-level device exchange. We also design a module coupler that connects adjacent robotic modules for multi-instrument actuation and improves rotational torque transmission through long, compliant instruments. Benchtop characterization of the proposed 2-DOF mechanism showed peak insertion force up to 5.3 N and rotational torque up to 8.4 mN<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\cdot$</tex-math></inline-formula>m, exceeding reported clinical requirements. Phantom studies demonstrated a two-module (4-DOF) configuration for diagnostic angiography and a three-module (6-DOF) configuration for aspiration thrombectomy, including successful clot aspiration in a neurovascular phantom.

title: A Blind Signal Detection Method Based on Constrained Dual-Cluster K-means for FSO Communications with Atmospheric Turbulence
authors: Zhenning Yi, Hao Zhou, Zhiyong Xu, Jianhua Li, Jingyuan Wang, Jiyong Zhao, Yang Su, Cheng Li, Han Zhou, Ying Du
publish: IEEE Communications Letters
abstract: Existing blind detection schemes for free-space optical (FSO) communications often suffer from bit error rate (BER) floors. These floors fundamentally arise from the misclassification of extreme signal blocks like all-0 and all-1 blocks. This letter proposes a robust blind signal detection method based on constrained dual-cluster K-means. The proposed method incorporates the physical properties of on-off keying modulation. It fixes the cluster center for the “0” symbol and iteratively optimizes only the “1” symbol center. Furthermore, the method integrates a temporal center retention mechanism to explicitly handle all-0 blocks. This combined strategy effectively prevents the center drift issue inherent in conventional unsupervised learning. Consequently, the proposed scheme completely eliminates the extreme-block-induced BER floor without requiring pilot overhead or prior channel statistics. Simulation results using measured intensity data confirm the superiority of this approach. The method outperforms existing blind detection schemes and achieves robust performance approaching ideal maximum likelihood (ML) detection. It also maintains a linear computational complexity suitable for real-time processing.

title: A Closed-Form Phase Design for RIS-Aided UCA-OAM Systems
authors: Zhongpeng Wang, Hongyang Han
publish: IEEE Communications Letters
abstract: Reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) phase optimization is essential for enhancing the capacity of RIS-aided uniform circular array (UCA) based orbital angular momentum (OAM) systems. In this letter, we propose a closed-form phase design that aligns the propagation paths of all OAM modes to maximize the total received power. The proposed scheme computes the RIS reflection coefficients without iteration, offering a low-complexity alternative to conventional iterative algorithms. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed design achieves near-optimal sum-rate performance across a wide range of RIS sizes and transmit powers, while iterative algorithms (e.g., DSM) provide marginal gains only under extreme high-SNR conditions. The proposed design also serves as an efficient initialization for more sophisticated optimizers.

title: Enhancing Privacy-Preserving Software Development from a Developers' Perspective: A Survey

title: Wavelet-Domain Masked Image Modeling for Color-Consistent HDR Video Reconstruction
authors: Yang Zhang, Zhangkai Ni, Wenhan Yang, Hanli Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: High Dynamic Range (HDR) video reconstruction aims to recover fine brightness, color, and details from Low Dynamic Range (LDR) videos. However, existing methods often suffer from color inaccuracies and temporal inconsistencies. To address these challenges, we propose WMNet, a novel HDR video reconstruction network that leverages Wavelet domain Masked Image Modeling (W-MIM). WMNet adopts a two-phase training strategy: In Phase I, W-MIM performs self-reconstruction pre-training by selectively masking color and detail information in the wavelet domain, enabling the network to develop robust color restoration capabilities. A curriculum learning scheme further refines the reconstruction process. Phase II fine-tunes the model using the pre-trained weights to improve the final reconstruction quality. To improve temporal consistency, we introduce the Temporal Mixture of Experts (T-MoE) module and the Dynamic Memory Module (DMM). T-MoE adaptively fuses adjacent frames to reduce flickering artifacts, while DMM captures long-range dependencies, ensuring smooth motion and preservation of fine details. Additionally, since existing HDR video datasets lack scene-based segmentation, we reorganize HDRTV4K into HDRTV4K-Scene, establishing a new benchmark for HDR video reconstruction. Extensive experiments demonstrate that WMNet achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple evaluation metrics, significantly improving color fidelity, temporal coherence, and perceptual quality.

title: ROBBO: An Efficient Method for Pareto Front Estimation With Guaranteed Accuracy
authors: Roberto Boffadossi, Marco Leonesio, Lorenzo Fagiano
publish: IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics: Systems
abstract: A new method to estimate the Pareto front (PF) in bi-objective optimization problems (BOPs) is presented. Assuming a continuous PF, the approach, named ROBBO (RObust and Balanced BOP), needs to sample at most a finite, precomputed number of PF points. Upon termination, it guarantees that the worst case approximation error lies within a desired tolerance range, predefined by the decision maker (DM), for each of the two objective functions. Theoretical results are derived about the worst case number of PF samples required to guarantee the desired accuracy, both in general and for specific sampling methods from the literature. A comparative analysis, both theoretical and numerical, demonstrates the superiority of the proposed method with respect to popular ones. The approach is finally showcased in a constrained path-following problem for a 2-axis positioning system and in a steady-state optimization problem for a continuous-flow stirred tank reactor (CSTR). An open demo implementation of ROBBO is made available online.

title: Stray Current Mitigation With Reverse Running Rail Current Control Strategy for Virtual-Return-Based DC Traction Power Supply System
authors: Miao Wang, Jingyue Lu, Wenzhong Ma, Hailiang Xu, Pingjuan Ge, Xiaofeng Yang, Trillion Q. Zheng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Delivery
abstract: The virtual-return-based DC traction power supply system (VRTPS) has been studied to solve the stray current and rail potential issues in urban rail transit. By utilizing the power electronic devices (PEDs) and return line, VRTPS constructs the zero-resistance virtual return path (VRP) by diverting traction current from the running rail into this newly established path. However, the mitigation performance of existing VRTPS control strategies is still suboptimal, leading to incomplete suppression of stray current and rail potential within the train-occupied section. Thus, the reverse running rail current control strategy of VRTPS is proposed to further reduce the stray current and rail potential. Firstly, the topology and modeling of VRTPS are investigated to analyze the inherent limitations of the ideal zero-resistance VRP. Secondly, the detailed analysis of the reverse running rail current control for VRTPS is presented, which optimizes the running rail current distribution with the variable-resistance VRP. Thirdly, the comparative researches on both the stray current and rail potential distribution are conducted between the conventional system and VRTPS with different control strategies. Finally, the correctness and effectiveness of VRTPS with proposed strategy on the stray current and rail potential mitigation are validated through simulation and experimental results.

title: A Data-Driven Algorithm for Indoor/Outdoor Detection Based on 3GPP MDT measures
authors: Giuliano Muratore, Davide Micheli, Roberto Verdone
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Using cellular networks outdoors generally leads to terminals experiencing elevated power levels and increased interference from neighboring cells. These conditions are significantly different from those encountered by the same terminals when they are indoors, where absorption caused by building walls reduces the available serving cell received power and mitigates interference from other cells. Conditions where mobile radio services are predominantly used by terminals located outdoors or, conversely, predominantly used within buildings, typically mix depending on the city area, the specific time, or peculiar circumstances. Therefore, the ability to track the evolution of the dominant indoor or outdoor context within a network over time is crucial for automatically adjusting, optimizing, and enhancing radiomobile service quality. Although numerous methods exist for estimating a device's indoor or outdoor status, based on specific positioning strategies, our approach stands apart due to its real-time applicability to a large-scale set of smartphones across any current 4G/5G 3GPP radiomobile network worldwide—something not achieved by other solutions in the literature. The proposed model is validated using an extensive dataset sourced from a live 5G network access.

title: A Hierarchical Carbon Emission Factor Correction Method Considering Non-Grid-Connected Electricity
authors: Dawei Ma, Haotian Zhang, Ming Kong, Chao Ji, Wei Tang, Yang Su, Bin Xu, Chao Liu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Accurate carbon emission factors (CEFs) are essential for tracking and reducing emissions in power systems. Traditional average CEF methods suffer from coarse granularity, while carbon flow tracing approaches are computationally intensive and unsuitable for large-scale grids. Moreover, both ignore the environmental benefits of non-grid-connected electricity (NGCE), such as local rooftop PV or self-consumed distributed generation. This paper proposes a hierarchical correction method for regional CEFs that accounts for NGCE. Based on electrical partitioning theory, the grid is divided into three layers: provincial, prefectural, and end-user. A recursive calculation strategy is adopted to compute CEFs for grid-connected electricity, while NGCE is attributed to local nodes for final correction. Case studies validate the method’s feasibility, showing improved spatiotemporal resolution and accurate reflection of distributed generation benefits. The approach offers a practical pathway for carbon accounting in regional power systems.

title: System-Level Coordinated Control Model of Energy Storage Systems for Grid Security and Stability Analysis
authors: Yuhan Liu, Hanyang Dai, Xiong Xiao, Xiangtao Xiao, Xinli Song, Shaohang Hao, Yikang Wang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: With the large-scale integration of renewable energy, energy storage systems are playing an increasingly critical role in enhancing grid security and operational flexibility. This paper presents a system-level coordinated control model for energy storage systems participating in grid security and stability control, suitable for large-scale power system simulation. The study first analyzes the functions of energy storage systems in power flow control, frequency regulation, and voltage support. Based on this, a coordinated control framework is developed, integrating automatic generation control, automatic voltage control, security and stability control systems, and a priority-based weight allocation mechanism that coordinates command priorities with resource availability. The proposed model is validated through a four-machine two-area system simulation for secondary frequency regulation and a cascading fault case study based on an actual provincial power grid. Results demonstrate that the model accurately captures the multi-timescale dynamic characteristics of utility-scale energy storage systems and effectively supports grid security and stability analysis.

title: Photoacoustic signal from hemoglobin target
authors: Isabel Nunes Ramalho da Rocha, Juliana Oliveira da Silva Fernandes, Raymundo Cordero Garcia, Krishynan Santhy Fernandes Meirelles Araujo, Beatriz Ferreira de Carvalho Patricio, Ana Beatriz Furlanetto Pacheco, João Carlos Machado
publish: 2026 Global Medical Engineering Physics Exchanges/ Pan American Health Care Exchanges (GMEPE/PAHCE)
abstract: Photoacoustic (PA) systems have been extensively studied for their potential to obtain functional information from biological organs and tissues. Studies report the potential of endogenous molecules, such as hemoglobin (Hb), to generate photoacoustic signals. This study presents an experimental evaluation of a PA system developed at the Ultrasound Laboratory of the Biomedical Engineering Program at COPPE/UFRJ. For this purpose, a rectangular phantom was constructed from a 2% agarose solution using a mold produced by 3D printing, which had internal channels molded by catheters, which were removed after the agarose solidified. Initially, graphite was analyzed, used as a positive control for optical absorption, followed by a solution of human Hb at a concentration of 1 mg/ml. Photoacoustic excitation was performed using a DPSS laser source operating at 1064 nm, and the acoustic signals generated were detected by an ultrasonic transducer. The results demonstrated the acquisition of clearly detectable photoacoustic signals for both absorbents, highlighting the sensitivity of the system to Hb detection, as found in the literature. This study validates the proposed experimental methodology and establishes a basis for future studies.

title: Tyndall-Effect-Based Optical Monitoring of Micro-Plastic Pollution in Aquatic Environments
authors: Victor Ababii, Viorica Sudacevschi, Galina Marusic, Viorel Carbune
publish: 2026 International Conference on Development and Application Systems (DAS)
abstract: The paper presents the development and validation of an experimental system for monitoring microplastic pollution in aquatic environments, based on the Tyndall effect and artificial intelligence techniques. The proposed system uses a laser light source and a video acquisition module to detect light scattered by microplastic particles, with data processed in real time on the NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano Developer Kit (SBC) platform. Mathematical models and discrete algorithms are developed for correlating the optical signal with the concentration of microplastics, and the performance of the method is improved by integrating artificial neural networks such as CNN and LSTM. The experimental results, presented in the form of images and quantitative graphs, demonstrate the system's ability to identify the presence of microplastics and dynamically monitor concentration variations. The proposed solution offers a fast, non-invasive, and scalable approach with high potential for continuous monitoring of the aquatic environment.

title: Optimization Strategy for Micro Energy Grid Transactions in Multi-Level Energy Markets Based on Asymmetric Nash Bargaining
authors: Shan Liu, Kouyu Sun, Yiheng Liang, Kangyang Lv, Chenhui Xu
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: With the advancement of the multi-level energy market, micro energy grid (MEG) operators are confronted with core issues such as insufficient transaction synergy and imbalance in the distribution of benefits among entities. This paper proposes a coordinated multi-level electricity market trading strategy and constructs an integrated multi-level energy trading framework covering the energy wholesale market, the peer-to-peer electricity market, and the shared energy storage leasing market. To balance efficiency and fairness in multi-level energy market coordination, an optimization method based on the asymmetric Nash bargaining theory is further developed. First, the strategy quantifies the actual contribution of each participating entity in multi-level transactions, and scientifically sets differentiated bargaining weights for all entities. On this basis, it realizes the global optimization of the overall operating cost of the MEG system while taking into account the benefit demands of each trading entity, thus achieving a reasonable and fair distribution of benefits. Finally, the alternating direction method of multipliers is adopted to solve the proposed optimization model, and case studies based on actual scenarios are conducted to verify the strategy. The results show that the proposed strategy can effectively improve the market coordination efficiency of the multi-level energy market, significantly reduce the comprehensive operating cost of the entire MEG system, and achieve a fair and reasonable distribution of trading benefits for each participating entity, which fully verifies the feasibility and effectiveness of the strategy.

title: Maintaining Lot Schedule Adherence through Overlapping Q-times via Admission Control
authors: Thomas Hewlett, Srikanth Ramakrishnan, Himanshu Mogra, Shiladitya Chakravorty, Detlef Pabst
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: Dispatching into a time-constrained sequence (Q-time or Time-link zone) poses a unique challenge for high volume manufacturing (HVM) factories, as schedule priority is, instead, often trumped by the protection of wafer quality. As fab processes mature, these constrained sequences may also continue to increase in complexity. This paper outlines a method by which both objectives of quality and schedule adherence can be achieved in an automated manner.

title: Unload Clean Removal for Showerhead Pattern Defects Improvement
authors: Hong Kai Tan, Kenneth Chih Yuang Mah, Ker Peng Lau, Edward See, Chek Chou Chiam
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: In CVD systems, clean recipe settings are closely related to the three core tenets of manufacturing, which are cost, productivity, and quality. In the interest of throughput, a cleaning configuration termed as "Clean on Unload Loadlock" may be utilized to process multiple wafers consecutively before the cleaning process is triggered to maximize output, which is synchronized to the wafer loading and unloading cycle. In this paper, we show that this cleaning configuration introduces a critical process imbalance. Specifically, in twin-reactor CVD systems, one chamber becomes repeatedly exposed to cyclic cleaning erosion, due to final wafers of a lot processing only in one chamber, leading to the partner chamber being uncoated during the subsequent cleaning and subjected to chemical attack by fluorine radicals. Hence, the removal of such cleaning configuration is proposed to mitigate showerhead pattern defects, with an improvement from an average of 4.6 cases per year to zero cases since implementation to date, and gas cost savings.

title: A Data-driven Approach to Synergistic Resilient Assessment and Investment Optimization for Sustainable Operation of Island Power Grids
authors: Yuetong Li, Haoyang Yu, Hongfan Li, Xueyong Ding, Shifeng Wang, Liansheng Wang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Island power grids face the core challenge of improving power supply reliability while controlling investment costs. To scientifically quantify the trade-off between these two factors and seek the optimal solution for social welfare, this paper constructs a data-driven resilience-economy synergistic assessment and investment decision optimization framework. Firstly, comprehensive system resilience metrics, total social costs, and marginal resilience costs are defined. Subsequently, a three-part methodology comprising power outage loss prediction, core metric calculation, and visualized decision-making is proposed. Finally, taking the Hainan power grid as a case study, five investment scenarios and three disaster severity levels are designed. The research demonstrates that there exists a clear optimal economic equilibrium point for grid resilience investment, with distinct patterns in marginal cost variations. Optimizing investment strategies can effectively enhance social welfare. This study provides a scientific quantitative analysis tool for refined grid planning and investment decision-making.

title: Electronic Fratricide in Air Defence Operations While Countering Uas in Tactical Battle Areas
authors: M Y Khan, Pranjal Agarwal, Rajagopal Chellamani Srikanth
publish: 2026 International Conference on Air Defense and Security (ICADS)
abstract: Electronic fratricide in air defence operations refers to the unintended interference, degradation or neutralisation of friendly sensors, communication networks, navigation aids and weapon control systems due to uncoordinated electronic emissions within a contested electromagnetic environment. The rapid proliferation of Unmanned Aerial Systems (UAS), operating across diverse frequency bands with low radar crosssections, has compelled air defence forces to increasingly rely on electronic countermeasures, jammers, spoofers and directed energy weapons[1] [2]. While these measures are essential for countering hostile UAS, inadequate spectrum coordination and decentralised control can inadvertently disrupt friendly systems, resulting in loss of situational awareness, delayed engagements and increased risk of blue-on-blue incidents[2] [4]. This paper analyses the evolution of the UAS threat, its employment in contemporary conflicts, existing counter-UAS measures and the primary causes of electronic fratricide. Case studies from recent conflicts are examined to highlight operational risks, followed by recommendations to mitigate electronic fratricide through centralised command and control, integrated spectrum management, training and procedural reforms.

title: Analysis and Prediction of Influencing Factors of Power Carbon Emissions in Guizhou Province Based on LMDI Decomposition and Multi-NAR Integrated Nonlinear Regression
authors: Chen Luo, Julong Chen, Xuepeng Mou, Bin Wang, Shiping Yang
publish: 2026 9th International Conference on Energy, Electrical and Power Engineering (CEEPE)
abstract: Understanding the drivers of power carbon emissions is essential for improving emission forecasting and supporting low-carbon energy planning. Taking Guizhou Province as a case study, this study analyzes and predicts power sector carbon emissions during 2007–2022. The logarithmic mean Divisia index method is applied to decompose emission changes into multiple driving effects. The results indicate that urban per capita GDP, electricity intensity, and population scale are the dominant drivers. The GDP effect accounts for about 59% of the emission growth, while improvements in electricity intensity offset nearly 47% of the increase. Based on these factors, NAR, NARX, and FNN models are employed for forecasting analysis. Rolling test results show prediction errors ranging from 12.89% to 15.31% MAPE, providing a reliable evaluation of carbon emission trends in Guizhou.

title: Study on Si-contamination in Developable BARC (DBARC) in High-K Metal Gate Process
authors: Wu Wenbin, Zhang Lei, Li Xiaojing, Fu Yong Feng
publish: 2026 37th Annual SEMI Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing Conference (ASMC)
abstract: This paper presents a study on the effect of a Si-containing DBARC chemical during lithography step in HKMG process. Compared to normal good batch and unopen bottle of same type of DBARC chemical, an additional thin film was observed if the Si-containing DBARC material was coated together with photoresist. The formation of such Si–O film on top of wafer surface later caused related device failure. Cross-section TEM and EDX mapping techniques were used to characterize the film. Further experiment of characterization of this thin film with SIMS analysis indicates a potential application of in-line SIMS tool to monitor contamination from DBARC.

title: Intelligent Modeling for Deriving Human Lens Stiffness Based on Patients’ Demographic Features with Outcome in Phacoemulsification Surgery
authors: Sara Rahmati, Hadi Tabesh
publish: 2025 11th International Conference on Signal Processing and Intelligent Systems (ICSPIS)
abstract: Background: Accurate preoperative assessment of human lens stiffness is a critical determinant in modern cataract surgery, particularly for optimizing ultrasound energy in phacoemulsification. Current clinical grading methods often rely on subjective judgment, which limits personalized surgical planning. Methods: We developed a predictive framework utilizing routine demographic and clinical features to estimate the gold-standard ex vivo elastic modulus (Young's modulus) of 50 cataractous human lenses, obtained via extracapsular cataract extraction (ECCE). A suite of supervised regression algorithms, including Support Vector Regression (SVR), was implemented and rigorously compared based on Mean Squared Error (MSE) and R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> metrics. Results: SVR achieved the highest predictive performance on the test set (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>=0.9409). Feature importance analysis consistently identified cataract grade (LOCS III), lens diameter, and age as the most influential factors driving stiffness prediction. Conclusion: This study establishes a successful intelligent proof-of-concept demonstrating the feasibility of correlating readily available clinical data with fundamental biomechanical properties using advanced machine learning. The models lay the necessary groundwork for developing non-invasive, AI-driven diagnostic tools to enhance surgical safety and efficiency.
